elements.sent-2411-002: Pebble accretion refers to the growth of planetary bodies through the accretion of pebble-sized particles.
elements.sent-2411-003: Pebbles are defined in terms of their aerodynamically size $\tau_s$, which describes the level of coupling to the disk gas.
elements.sent-2411-004: Observations confirms the presence of pebble-sized particles in both protoplanetary disks and the early solar system.
elements.sent-2411-005: Pebble accretion proceeds through the settling mechanism, where particles settle to the surface of the planet.
elements.sent-2411-006: This Chapter discusses the key aspects of the pebble accretion framework: the accretion regimes, the planet initiation mass, and the planet isolation masses.
elements.sent-2411-007: The accretion behavior of loosely coupled $\tau_s>1$ particles, referred to as ``large pebbles'', is also examined.
elements.sent-2411-008: The pebble accretion probability, $\epsilon$, is shown to be a useful parameter for evaluating the efficiency of the process, though this quantity is not necessarily high.
elements.sent-2411-009: Distinctions between pebble and planetesimal accretion are outlined.
elements.sent-2411-010: Pebble accretion, in particular, can be a highly effective mechanism in dense rings, as witnessed with ALMA.
elements.sent-2411-011: Pebble accretion is a process that describes the growth of large bodies (planetesimals or planets) by accretion of small, aerodynamically active particles.
elements.sent-2411-012: It should not be confused with processes that describe the formation of macroscopic bodies itself, which could also involve pebble-sized particles.
elements.sent-2411-013: In particular, the \hbindex{streaming instability (or other drag-mediated instabilities) describe how planetesimal size bodies form by concentration and subsequent gravitational collapse.}
elements.sent-2411-014: But this is not pebble accretion.
elements.sent-2411-015: Before introducing pebble accretion we first must clarify what pebbles are in an astrophysical context.
elements.sent-2411-016: I will define \begin{svgraybox Pebbles are aerodynamically active particles that can drift over significant distances within the lifetime of the protoplanetary disk \end{svgraybox In the next, I review the observational evidence for the existence of pebble-sized particles in disks and the aerodynamics of particles in gas-rich disks.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Then I will intro the key physical underpinning pebble accretion and the framework as it has developed over the past decade.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Observational Evidence for Pebbles in Disks
elements.sent-2411-018: In protoplanetary disks, pebbles settle to the midplane regions due to the vertical component of the stellar gravity.
elements.sent-2411-019: The midplane regions of the disk would typically be inaccessible with facilities that operate at optical and near-IR wavelengths (including JWST).
elements.sent-2411-020: It is only at radio wavelength that the midplane regions are revealed.
elements.sent-2411-021: Here, ALMA has revolutionized the field by spatially resolving disks, showing a variety of substructure in the forms of spirals, vortices, and, especially, rings.
elements.sent-2411-022: This substructure is best exhibited at continuum wavelengths, which traces the underlying solids population.
elements.sent-2411-023: The prevalence of substructure offers us with the first line of evidence for the dominance of pebble-sized particles in disks.
elements.sent-2411-024: Unless substructure can directly be generated from infalling material (Calcino et al., in prep) only pebble-sized particles would have the aerodynamical properties to migrate over the distances required to display the substructure.
elements.sent-2411-025: A straightforward example of this argument is that disks tend to be seen significantly smaller in continuum than in the gas \citep{SanchisEtal2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: The explanation is that pebble-sized particles have vacated the outer disks, drifting ${\sim 10\,\mathrm{au}$ inwards.}
elements.sent-2411-027: A more subtle example is that pebble-sized particles, would strongly react to any local pressure perturbation, i.e., pebbles pile-up in pressure maxima.
elements.sent-2411-028: Even when the magnitude of the pressure perturbation that lies behind the reversal is small, $|\delta P|/P \ll 1$, it would greatly amplify the imprint on the pebble disk.
elements.sent-2411-029: The at times spectacular substructure seen in ALMA continuum requires aerodynamically active particles.
elements.sent-2411-030: The second line of evidence for the existence of pebbles, is the spectral dependence of the observed emission $I_\nu$ at sub(mm)/radio wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-031: This dependence arises from the size dependence of the opacity $\kappa_\nu$---the absorption cross section per unit solid mass.
elements.sent-2411-032: In the Interstellar Medium (ISM), grains are thought to be small, reaching sizes up to ${\sim 0.1\,\mu\mathrm{m}$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: At sub(mm) wavelengths the Rayleigh limit is applicable and the opacity decreases with wavelength, $\kappa_\nu \propto \nu^{\beta}$.
elements.sent-2411-035: On the other hand, particles of size much larger than the wavelength would not exhibit a wavelength dependence ($\beta=0$).
elements.sent-2411-036: Hence, grain growth would manifest itself as a decrease in $\beta$.

elements.sent-2411-038: If the temperature is known (and under the optically thin assumption) measuring $\alpha$ informs us on the sizes of the underlying particles.
elements.sent-2411-039: Indeed, studies indicate $\beta$ values substantially smaller than the ISM value, providing evidence for grain growth to pebble sizes \citep{TestiEtal2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Although there are many complicating factors to this simple reasoning (optical depth and scattering effects, temperature dependence, dependence of material properties and internal structure) the consensus is that the change in $\alpha$ reflects grain growth \citep{SierraEtal2021,Guerra-AlvaradoEtal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The inferred disk mass in pebbles for standard T-Tauri disks are quite small, perhaps only several Earth masses \citep{AnsdellEtal2017 , but it is considerably higher for younger Class 0/I disks \citep{TychoniecEtal2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Other complementary evidence for the presence of grain growth comes from millimeter polarization measurements in disks \citep{KataokaEtal2015,YangEtal2016 and from the meteoritic record in the solar system.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In the latter, undifferentiated (chondritic) meteorites are often stuffed with chondrules: ${\sim 300\,\mu\mathrm{m}$-sized particles.}
elements.sent-2411-044: According to the Udden-Wentworth scale pebbles are particles between 4 and 64 mm in diameter \citep{WilliamsEtal2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Pebbles in this context are neither defined in terms of size nor necessarily round.
elements.sent-2411-046: Instead, the implied meaning of pebble is aerodynamical: pebbbles are particles that couple in a certain way to the gas.
elements.sent-2411-047: Particles moving through a gaseous medium experience gas drag.
elements.sent-2411-048: The amount of gas drag is customarily expressed in terms a drag coefficient $C_D$: \begin{equation}\bm{F}_\mathrm{drag} = -\frac{1}{2} C_D A \rho_\mathrm{gas} v\bm{v} \label{eq:Fdrag}\end{equation} where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the particle, $\bm{v}$ the velocity of the particle with respect to the gas, and $\rho_\mathrm{gas}$ its density.
elements.sent-2411-049: The lower the $C_D$, the more economical your sports car or jet aircraft.
elements.sent-2411-050: At low densities, there are two notes to make.
elements.sent-2411-051: The first is that the drag coefficient $C_D$ in \eq{Fdrag is not constant.}
elements.sent-2411-052: In the literature one can find expressions for how $C_D$ depends on the flow Reynolds number $\mathrm{Re _p = 2sv/\nu_\mathrm{mol}$ where $\nu_\mathrm{mol}$ is the molecular viscosity and $s$ the particle radius (see e.g., \citealt{Weidenschilling1997 for spheres).}}
elements.sent-2411-053: For $\mathrm{Re _p<1$ drag is linear in velocity.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In addition, if the distances between gas molecules (their mean free path $l_\mathrm{mfp}$) exceeds the particle size $s$, drag follows the specular reflection limit\? \begin{equation}\bm{F}_\mathrm{drag} = - \frac{4\pi}{3}s^2 \rho_\mathrm{gas} v_\mathrm{th} \bm{v} \qquad (s<\frac{9}{4}l_\mathrm{mfp}) \label{eq:Fdrag-molecular}\end{equation} where $v_\mathrm{th =\sqrt{8kT/\pi m_\mathrm{gas }$ is the gas mean thermal motion.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: In this limit drag is also linear in velocity.
elements.sent-2411-056: This means that a particle's \hbindex{stopping time $t_\mathrm{stop}\equiv mv/|F_D|$ becomes independent of velocity: \begin{equation}t_\mathrm{stop} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l@{\qquad}l@{\qquad}l} \displaystyle \frac{\rho_\bullet s}{\rho_\mathrm{gas} v_\mathrm{th}} s<\frac{9}{4}l_\mathrm{mfp} Epstein [1.4em] \displaystyle \frac{\rho_\bullet s}{\rho_\mathrm{gas} v_\mathrm{th}} \left( \frac{4s}{9l_\mathrm{mfp}} \right) \frac{9}{4}l_\mathrm{mfp}<s< s[\mathrm{Re}_p=1] Stokes \end{array} \right. \label{eq:tstop}\end{equation} The independence of stopping time on velocity is the reason $t_\mathrm{stop}$ is used to quantify the aerodynamical interaction of a particle.}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}t_stop = \left\{ {l@{\qquad}l@{\qquad}l} \displaystyle \frac{\rho_\bullet s}{\rho_gas v_th} s<\frac{9}{4}l_mfp Epstein [1.4em] \displaystyle \frac{\rho_\bullet s}{\rho_gas v_th} \left( \frac{4s}{9l_mfp} \right) \frac{9}{4}l_mfp<s< s[Re_p=1] Stokes  \right. \label{eq:tstop}}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-057: Often the stopping time is nondimensionalized with respect to the orbital frequency: \begin{equation}\tau_s = t_\mathrm{stop}\Omega_K.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: Thus, $\tau_s<1$ indicates particles circularize their motion within one orbital period, while $\tau_s>1$ particles do not.
elements.sent-2411-059: The latter could be described in the framework of Kepler's orbit with gas drag imposing\? a perturbative force.
elements.sent-2411-060: For $\tau_s\ll1$ the Kepler representation becomes meaningless; $\tau_s\ll1$ particles closely follow any gas motion.
elements.sent-2411-061: We will see below that $\tau_s=1$ are the fastest drifters.
elements.sent-2411-062: Pebbles are hence defined in terms of the aerodynamical stopping time $\tau_s$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Depending on the situation we may define pebbles as $10^{-2 \le \tau_s \le 10^2$ particles or perhaps $10^{-3 \le \tau_s \le 10^3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: We will refer to pebbles $\tau_s>1$ as \hbindex{large pebbles .}
elements.sent-2411-065: With this definition, any particle in the absence of gas (such that $\tau_s\rightarrow\infty$) is not a pebble.
elements.sent-2411-066: Protoplanetary disks are supported against the stellar gravity by rotation and pressure $P$.
elements.sent-2411-067: If rotation would be the only support mechanism (i.e., no pressure gradient) the orbital velocity of the gas would equal the Keplerian velocity $v_{\phi,\mathrm{gas = v_K = \sqrt{Gm_\star/r}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: In general, the radial pressure gradient (usually negative) provides some hydrostatic support, such that that the gas moves at a velocity $v_\mathrm{\phi,gas}\simeq (1-\eta)v_K$ where $\eta$ is defined \begin{equation}\eta \equiv - \frac{1}{2\Omega_K^2 r \rho_\mathrm{gas}} \frac{\partial P}{\partial r} \sim \frac{P}{v_K^2 \rho_\mathrm{gas}} \sim h_\mathrm{gas}^2. \label{eq:eta}\end{equation} Here, $\partial P/\partial r$ has been assumed to be some power-law of radius, $h_\mathrm{gas =c_s/v_K$ is the gas aspect ratio, and $c_s$ the isothermal sound speed.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Hence, $\eta\ll1$; the gas rotates at near-Keplerian speeds with a departing from Keplerian motion by $\eta v_K \sim 10{- 100\, \mathrm{m\,s^{-1 }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Particles do not feel the gas pressure ($\rho_\bullet \gg \rho_\mathrm{gas}$) but they do interact with the gas aerodynamically through drag forces.
elements.sent-2411-071: Gas friction extracts angular momentum from the particles, which therefore tend to drift in radially.
elements.sent-2411-072: Solving for the steady solutions to the Euler equations for gas and particles simultaneously, we find that the drift velocities, in a frame co-moving at the Keplerian orbital velocity are \citep{NakagawaEtal1986 : \begin{equation}\label{eq:vdrift} v_r = -\frac{2\tau_s}{\tau_s^2 +(1+Z_\mathrm{mid})^2} \eta v_K; \qquad v_\phi = -\frac{(1+Z_\mathrm{mid})}{\tau_s^2 +(1+Z_\mathrm{mid})^2} \eta v_K\end{equation} where $Z_\mathrm{mid}$ is the local (midplane) solids-to-gas ratio.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Since we will typically assume in this chapter that a planet moves on a circular orbit these are the velocities at which the planet faces the pebbles (see \fg{geom ).}
elements.sent-2411-074: For small $\tau_s$ and $Z_\mathrm{mid}\approx0$ we have that $v_\phi\simeq -\eta v_K$, which expresses that pebbles move along with the gas, and $v_r\simeq -2\eta v_K \tau_s$.
elements.sent-2411-075: The radial drift peaks at $\tau_s=1$ at which point the drift timescale $t_\mathrm{drift =r/|v_r| \sim 1/\eta\Omega_K$, is just several hundreds of orbital periods.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The drift velocities decrease for either $\tau_s\gg1$ or $Z_\mathrm{mid}\gg1$.
elements.sent-2411-077: In the case where $\tau_s<1$ but $Z_\mathrm{mid}\gg1$ this happens because the tightly-coupled particles drag the gas along to Keplerian rotation.

The planet moves on a circular orbit in the positive azimuthal direction (leftwards), facing pebbles at a relative velocity $v_\phi$---the headwind term. elements.sent-2411-078: I will give two definitions of pebble accretion: \begin{svgraybox.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Pebble accretion is an accretion mechanism where a planet accretes pebble---particles that drift significantly (as defined above).
elements.sent-2411-080: Like above, but where in addition the pebbles are captured in the planet's gravitational well and settle to the surface of the planet.
elements.sent-2411-081: I will describe these settling trajectories first, before outlining the region in the planet mass--pebble size parameter space where we can find these.

elements.section-2411-004: Pebble accretion as a planet formation mechanism
elements.sent-2411-233: Having established the basis of pebble accretion, we now turn to its ability to grow planets.
elements.sent-2411-234: A useful metric is the pebble accretion efficiency, which directly informs us on the required mass budget of pebbles.
elements.sent-2411-235: I will then discuss the different characteristics of pebble accretion in comparison to planetesimal accretion.
elements.sent-2411-236: Pebble accretion is not necessarily efficient.
elements.sent-2411-237: With this I mean that the probability of a pebble to be accreted: \begin{equation}\epsilon \simeq \frac{\dot{m}}{\dot{M}_\mathrm{flux}} = \mathcal{R}_\mathrm{peb} \times \frac{v_K}{2\pi v_r} \label{eq:epsilon}\end{equation} can be low even though the rate ($\dot{m}$) is high.
elements.sent-2411-238: For example, when $\dot{M _\mathrm{flux =10^3\,\mathrm{m_\oplus\,Myr^{-1 }$, and $\epsilon=0.01$ a planet of 10 Earth mass doubles its mass after 1 Myr.}}}
elements.sent-2411-239: But during this time an amount $990\,m_\oplus$ in pebbles have drifted past the planets and are no longer available to its growth.
elements.sent-2411-240: This loss would be ameliorated if multiple embryos reside in the disk, but $\epsilon$ still captures the viability of pebble accretion to produce planets.
elements.sent-2411-241: After all, for planet formation to be successful, the preferred situation is that a few bodies consumes most of the pebbles, not that all pebbles get distributed over thousands of embryos.

elements.sent-2411-297: With this concise review I aim to have clarified the following:.
elements.sent-2411-298: Pebbles are defined in terms of the dimensionless stopping time $\tau_s$: its aerodynamical size.
elements.sent-2411-299: Centered around $\tau_s=1$, they are particles that drift over significant distances in disks.
elements.sent-2411-300: There is observational evidence that a large fraction of the solid mass budget in disks once existed in the form of pebble-sized particles.
elements.sent-2411-301: Pebble accretion requires a dissipative medium (gas).
elements.sent-2411-302: In the settling mechanism, particles are captured within the Hill sphere of the planet, from which they cannot escape.
elements.sent-2411-303: This contrast ballistic accretion, which relies on hitting a surface.
elements.sent-2411-304: The settling mechanism requires masses above an initiation mass $m_\mathrm{init}$ \eqp{minit to ensure that encounters are sufficiently long.}
elements.sent-2411-305: Pebble accretion (settling) is precluded below $m_\mathrm{init}$ and the settling condition will also start to fail for $\tau_s>1$.
elements.sent-2411-306: There is an abrupt change in the accretion rate at $m_\mathrm{init}$.
elements.sent-2411-307: Pebble accretion cross sections can be large, but in smooth disks growth is not necessarily efficient due to the strong drift of pebbles.
elements.sent-2411-308: A useful quantity to describe pebble accretion is the accretion probability $\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-309: The pebble accretion rate is determined by the approach velocity $\Delta v$, the planet mass, $\tau_s$, and the pebble scaleheight.
elements.sent-2411-310: These properties determine a variety of regimes (2D vs 3D; shear vs headwind), summarized in \fg{regimes and \Tb{PA-rates .}}
elements.sent-2411-311: Accretion of aerodynamically large pebbles ($\tau_s>1$ but still drifting significantly) can become very effective as these particles settle to the midplane and approach at low velocity.
elements.sent-2411-312: Early planet atmospheres further boosts the accretion probability.
elements.sent-2411-313: In realistic situations, pebble accretion is likely to operate alongside planetesimal accretion.
elements.sent-2411-314: In the outer disk pebble accretion, once it is operational, is arguably more effective.
elements.sent-2411-315: Dense particulate rings, arising from local pressure maxima or particle pileups---likely the conditions of continuum rings seen with ALMA---can spawn planets through pebble accretion at large distances (several tens of au).
elements.sent-2411-316: Pebbles are defined in terms of their aerodynamical size $\tau_s$, which describes the level of coupling to the disk gas.
elements.sent-2411-317: Pebble accretion describes the growth of large bodies, ranging from planetesimals to planets, through the accretion of small, aerodynamically active particles \citep{OrmelKlahr2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-318: In this process, the planetary body is massive enough to gravitationally attract solid particles, but not yet massive enough to rapidly accrete gas.
elements.sent-2411-319: Pebbles are particles of intermediate size: small enough for gas drag to play a decisive role during the encounter but large enough to decouple from the gas.
elements.sent-2411-320: Pebble accretion provides a means to bridge the gap between ${\sim}$km-sized planetesimal bodies and the ${\sim 10\,m_\oplus$ mass at which runaway gas accretion commences.}
